narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44.7% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This model looks at key aspects of your background—like your early academic rankings, test scores, grades, family income, and demographics—to forecast bar passage success. In your case, you ranked in the lowest decile for your school's Year 1 grades and stayed there for Year 3, well below the typical student's mid-range position around the fifth or sixth decile. Your LSAT score of 34 also fell short of the average 36.7 seen in stronger profiles, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 was slightly under the usual 3.21. With a middle-class family income, your identity as a female student of Asian background, and plans to study full-time—which is common for most applicants—the model pieced together a concerning picture.

The strongest driver of this prediction was your bottom-decile Year 1 ranking, which had the biggest pull toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind it was your similarly low Year 3 decile, reinforcing that early and later academic struggles weighed heavily. Your LSAT score came next in influence, nudging the outlook further downward, followed by your middle income bracket, Asian racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender, which had the smallest roles—all subtly pushing in the same direction rather than helping. Compared to students who typically pass the bar on their first try, your academic markers stand out as notably lower, while your demographic and enrollment choices align more closely with the norm but couldn't offset those gaps.

While past grades and test scores can't be changed now, you can realistically boost your future bar passage odds by targeting preparation strategies that address academic weaknesses—such as intensive LSAT retakes if eligible, supplemental coursework to build GPA momentum, or targeted tutoring to mimic higher-decile performance. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by focusing on disciplined study habits and bar-specific prep courses early on. Your full-time commitment is a solid foundation, and with targeted improvements, you could shift closer to the profiles of reliable first-time passers. Hang in there—this is just one data point, and your determination can rewrite the story.
